subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graphs use correct headings, units and significant figures, making communication both clear and precise
Uncertainties for resistance, dimensions and resistivity are calculated and propagated appropriately
Best- and worst-fit lines are used rigorously to gauge uncertainty
weaknesses: Temperature measurement uncertainty is not explicitly treated or propagated
Small typographic and rounding inconsistencies and a missing numerical determination of the temperature coefficient Î±
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data recording, processing and uncertainty analysis are carried out to a very high standard, with only minor omissions (temperature uncertainty treatment, Î± calculation) preventing faultless execution, justifying the top mark.

strengths: Research question positioned within a specific physical context (metallic conduction) and linked to electronics applications
Methodology is clearly described with materials, apparatus diagrams and a numbered procedure enabling reproducibility
Key methodological controls (length, cross-section, temperature equilibration, multiple trials) are identified and justified
weaknesses: Minor omissions in calibration details (thermometer, contact probes) and ambient control (humidity, drift)
Does not explain why the chosen number of trials and temperature points are sufficient for statistical reliability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The design is highly appropriate: the question is set in a clear physical and technological context, methods are reproducible and relevant considerations are discussed. Only minor omissions prevent a fuller top-band explanation, warranting a near-maximum score.

strengths: Conclusion is fully consistent with the analysis and justified by reference to the best-fit line and residuals
Qualitative comparison to the accepted linear resistivityâtemperature law is clearly made and supported by textbook references
weaknesses: No quantitative comparison of the experimentally determined temperature coefficient Î± with literature values
Lacks numerical benchmarking against published data
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is well supported and appropriately contextualized, satisfying the 5â6 descriptor. It falls short of the highest mark only due to the absence of a quantitative literature comparison.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (contact resistance, thermal lag, human error) are identified and their relative impacts on scatter and systematic shifts are explained
Realistic and relevant improvements (digital thermometer, better contacts, automated logging) are described
weaknesses: Improvements are not quantified in terms of how much they would reduce uncertainties
No estimation of the magnitude of improvement for each proposed change
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation pinpoints concrete limitations and proposes relevant improvements, with clear explanation of their impact. A full quantification of improvement effects would push this to the absolute top band, so the work sits securely at the 5â6 level.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student positions the question within the well-defined context of metallic conduction, quoting the linear resistivity-temperature law, linking it to electronics applications and to specific literature.  This goes beyond a mere statement yet remains focused on a single generic metal rather than, for example, a particular component or real-world device, so the description is highly appropriate but not exceptionally rich in breadth.

Uncertainties are calculated for R, d, A, L and propagated to Ï; worst-fit lines are used to gauge uncertainty in Î±.  Temperature uncertainty is not treated and some percentage values are rounded inconsistently, yet overall the consideration is appropriate and goes well beyond âlimitedâ or âsomeâ treatment.

Key considerations â control of length, cross-section, ambient conditions, multiple trials and thermal equilibrium time â are described and justified.  The student does not fully explain why five trials or eight temperature points are sufficient, nor are calibration or self-heating effects explored in depth, so the discussion is solid but not explanatory enough for the top band.

Several specific weaknessesâtemperature fluctuations, contact resistance, human reading error, residual heatingâare identified and the impact on scatter or systematic shifts is clearly explained, satisfying the requirement for explanation rather than mere description.

Materials, quantities, apparatus diagrams and a numbered procedure are given, enabling another student to reproduce the investigation.  Nonetheless, some details are omitted or vague (e.g. precise method for fixing probe contacts, calibration of thermometer, way diameter was measured).  These minor ambiguities place the work comfortably in the 3-4 band rather than fully exhaustive.

Means, propagated uncertainties and linear fits are correctly performed, but occasional formula repetition errors (Ïâ definition, unit slips) and a missing numerical determination of Î± introduce noticeable inaccuracies.  Hence the processing is sound with some significant omissions, fitting the 3-4 band.

Improvements such as using a digital thermometer, clamps for contacts, automated logging and extended cooling are realistic and relevant; however, the discussion stops at description and does not fully explain how much each change would reduce the quantified uncertainty, so it fits the âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ level.

Raw-data and processed-data tables use correct headings, units and significant figures; graphs are labelled and include best/worst fits.  Minor typographic glitches do not impede clarity or precision, therefore communication meets the 5-6 descriptor, though not perfect enough to justify the absolute maximum mark.

The student links results qualitatively to the accepted linear law and cites textbook references, but does not calculate the experimental Î± or compare it numerically with published values for copper.  Thus there is some relevant comparison, not yet a justified one.

The stated conclusion (Ï rises linearly with T) is fully consistent with the plotted data and statistical treatment, and is explicitly supported by reference to the best-fit line and small deviations.  The justification is persuasive, though quantitative comparison to literature Î± is absent, so a full 6 is not warranted.

annotations.comment: The title clearly states the focus of the investigation and frames the study topic succinctly, aiding reader understanding.
The research question is stated but lacks a specific context (e.g., choice of copper wire or a device application). Consider situating it within a particular material or real-world component.
To fully meet topâband Research Design, explain why this specific research question matters and cite a gap in the literature or technology to motivate the study.
The introduction references fundamental theory (Hummel, Ashcroft) to contextualize resistivityâtemperature dependence, providing solid theoretical grounding.
The background repeats the same equation twice (eqn 1 appears again as eqn 12). Consider consolidating or referencing it only once to avoid redundancy.
The hypothesis is clearly stated and follows logically from theory, linking atomic vibrations to increased resistivity.
Control variables are listed but not all methods for maintaining them (e.g., calibration of thermometer, probe contact consistency) are specified. Add these details to ensure reproducibility.
The controlledâvariables table outlines key parameters but misses ambient temperature and humidity controlâinclude these to strengthen methodological rigor.
The materials table lists sunflower oil and thermometer but omits their precision and calibration details, introducing uncertainty in measurement.
The stepâbyâstep method is wellâstructured and numbered, enabling others to follow the procedure in the lab.
It isnât specified how thermal equilibrium is verified beyond a timer. Consider measuring wire temperature directly to confirm equilibrium and justify the 5 min wait.
The procedure description âincrease by 10 Â° comesâ is unclearârevise for clarity (e.g., âincrease bath temperature by 10 Â°Câ).
The risk assessment is comprehensive and addresses electrical, thermal, and chemical hazards, demonstrating strong safety planning.
Rawâdata at 22 Â°C shows a wide spread (6.1â10.4 Î©), indicating potential contact resistance or timing errorsâaddress this in uncertainty analysis.
Processedâdata table uses correct significant figures and labels, making the presentation clear and precise.
Uncertainty in temperature measurement is not treated. Include the thermometerâs precision and propagate its uncertainty into resistivity.
Example calculations for mean and uncertainty are detailed and follow correct formulas, aiding reproducibility.
Diameter and area values are identical at each temperatureâmeasuring once and noting constancy suffices, rather than repeating the same table rows.
Resistivity calculation table (Table 6) is well organized, presenting resistivity and propagated uncertainties clearly.
The lines of best and worst fit are plotted but the temperature coefficient Î± is not numerically derived or compared to literatureâadd these computations.
Graph description notes how gradient relates to Î±, showing understanding of analysis and theoretical application.
Uncertainty table (Table 7) rounds some values inconsistently (e.g., 10% vs 9% at 50 Â°C) â ensure consistent rounding rules to avoid confusion.
The conclusion restates the hypothesis and aligns qualitatively with results, but omits quantitative comparison (e.g., experimental Î± vs literature Î±).
The evaluation table identifies realistic improvements (digital thermometer, clamps), but does not explain how much each change could reduce uncertaintyâquantify impacts if possible.
Listing strengths and limitations side by side effectively summarises methodological reliability and weaknesses for clear evaluation.
The suggestion to test different metals and extreme temperatures broadens scope and connects to real applications, enhancing future research relevance.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
rubricData.name: Physics IA
